C:
Short version: don't touch the paywall yet. Weak activation means people aren't reaching first real value, and optimizing monetization on top of that is pouring water into a leaky bucket. Define an activation metric, find the single biggest drop-off in the first-session funnel, and fix time-to-first-workout before anything downstream.

Here's how I'd actually go about it.

**1. Define activation as one measurable moment.** For a fitness app this is almost never "signed up" — it's the first completed workout, and better still the leading indicator of week-4 retention. Pull the data: find the early behavior that best predicts who's still around (and paying) a month later. It's usually something like *3 completed workouts in the first 7 days*. That number becomes the thing you optimize. Everything else is a vanity metric.

**2. Instrument the funnel and find the leak before building anything.** The honest first step is measurement, not redesign:

- Install → onboarding started
- Onboarding completed
- First workout *started*
- First workout *completed* ← the real activation gate
- Return for session 2 (next 1–3 days)
- Habit formed (3+ sessions / week 1)
- Trial/paywall → paid

The biggest single drop tells you where to spend. My strong prior: the two leaks are (a) onboarding-to-first-workout-completed, and (b) the session-1-to-session-2 return. Confirm which is bigger with the actual numbers.

**3. The fixes, by stage.**

- *Onboarding:* Cut it to the bone. Long surveys and account-creation walls before any value are the classic activation killer. Ask only what personalizes the very first workout (goal, level, equipment, time available), defer the account/email to *after* the first session, and let them feel progress within ~60 seconds. Use the inputs to drop them into a concrete first workout, not a generic library.
- *Time-to-first-value:* The first session should be short, winnable, and obviously tailored ("Your 12-minute starter, no equipment"). A completed easy workout beats an abandoned ambitious one. End it with a visible win and a single clear next action.
- *The return (session 2):* This is where most fitness apps die. Schedule the next workout *during* session 1 ("when's your next one — tomorrow 7am?"), tie a reminder/notification to that commitment, and open a light streak/progress loop. The goal is a specific planned return, not a generic "come back" push.
- *Only then, monetization:* Time the paywall to fire after the user has felt value (post first or second completed workout), and prefer a trial that's gated on activation rather than a hard wall at install.

**Where to focus first:** instrument the funnel this week so you're deciding on data, but if you want my bet on where the fix lands — **first completed workout in session one, then the engineered return to session two.** That pair is the highest-leverage part of activation and sits entirely upstream of the paywall, so improving it lifts trial-starts and conversion for free. Touch pricing and the paywall last.

One caveat worth checking early: if your acquisition is bringing in poorly-matched users, the funnel will look like an activation problem when it's really a targeting problem. Glance at activation rate segmented by channel before you commit — if one source is dragging the average down, that's a cheaper fix than any redesign.

If you can share the actual funnel numbers or what the current onboarding looks like, I'll point to the specific step to attack first instead of the general bet.

Touch pricing and the paywall last.

**The #1 test to run first:** strip onboarding down to the four personalization questions and drop the user straight into a single tailored ~10-minute first workout — account/email creation deferred to after it completes — measured as an A/B test against the current flow, with **first-workout-completion rate in session one** as the primary metric and session-2 return as the secondary. I'm starting here rather than the return fix because completion is the upstream gate (there's no session 2 to win if session 1 never completes), and because removing the account wall is the cheapest, fastest change with the clearest expected lift. **Hold for later, in this order:** the engineered session-2 return loop (scheduled next workout + commitment-tied reminder) as test #2 once completion is moving, then paywall timing and trial structure last — don't run pricing experiments until activation is fixed, or you'll be tuning conversion on traffic that was never going to stick. One exception that jumps the queue: if the segmented data below shows a single bad acquisition channel, fix that targeting before any onboarding test.

Glance at activation rate segmented by channel before you commit — if one source is dragging the average down, that's a cheaper fix than any redesign.

If you can share the actual funnel numbers or what the current onboarding looks like, I'll point to the specific step to attack first instead of the general bet.
P:
Short version: don't touch the paywall yet. Weak activation means people aren't reaching first real value, and tuning monetization on top of that is pouring water into a leaky bucket. Define what "activated" means, instrument the first-session funnel, find the single biggest drop-off, and fix time-to-first-completed-workout before anything downstream. My bet on where the fix lands — and where I'd start the day the data confirms it — is the first completed workout in session one, then the engineered return to session two.

Here's how I'd work it.

**1. Pin activation to two distinct moments — don't conflate them.**
- The *activation event* (the "aha") is almost never "signed up." For a fitness app it's the **first completed workout**. That's the gate you optimize.
- The *success metric* (your north star) is the early behavior that best predicts week-4 retention and paid conversion. Pull the data and find it; for fitness it usually lands near **3 completed workouts in the first 7 days**. That's how you'll know the work is paying off — not installs or signups, which are vanity here.

Optimize the event; judge yourself against the metric.

**2. Instrument the funnel before you build anything.** The honest first step is measurement, not redesign. Track each step and the conversion between them:
- Install
- Onboarding started
- Onboarding completed
- First workout started
- First workout completed (the activation gate)
- Return for session 2 (within 1–3 days)
- Habit: 3+ sessions in week 1
- Trial / paywall to paid

The biggest single drop tells you where the money is. My strong prior is that the two leaks are onboarding-to-first-completed-workout and session-1-to-session-2 return. Confirm which is larger with real numbers before you commit engineering.

**3. The fixes, by stage.**
- *Onboarding:* Cut it to the bone. Long surveys and an account/email wall before any value are the classic activation killer. Ask only what personalizes the first workout — goal, level, equipment, time available — and defer account creation until *after* the first session. The user should feel progress within about 60 seconds, and those inputs should drop them straight into a concrete first workout, not a generic library.
- *Time-to-first-value:* The first session should be short, winnable, and obviously tailored — "Your 12-minute starter, no equipment." A completed easy workout beats an abandoned ambitious one. End on a visible win and a single clear next action.
- *The return (session 2):* This is where most fitness apps die. Schedule the next workout *during* session 1 ("when's your next one — tomorrow, 7am?"), tie the reminder to that specific commitment, and open a light streak/progress loop. You want a planned return, not a generic "come back" push.
- *Monetization, last:* Fire the paywall only after the user has felt value — post first or second completed workout — and prefer a trial gated on activation over a hard wall at install.

**Where to focus first.** This week, instrument the funnel so you're deciding on data. In parallel, attack the first completed workout in session one (onboarding plus time-to-value), then the engineered return to session two. That pair is the highest-leverage part of activation and sits entirely upstream of the paywall, so improving it lifts trial-starts and paid conversion for free. You'll see it move in three numbers, in this order: first-workout-completion rate, then session-2 return rate, then downstream trial-starts. Pricing and the paywall come last.

**One thing to rule out first.** If acquisition is bringing in poorly-matched users, the funnel will read as an activation problem when it's really a targeting problem. Before any redesign, segment activation rate by channel — if one source is dragging the average down, cutting or fixing that spend is far cheaper than any redesign.

If you can share the actual funnel numbers or a screen-by-screen of the current onboarding, I'll point you to the specific step to attack instead of the general bet.
